text: This video is sponsored by Skillshare.

As most of you probably know, Saturday Night
Live is an American sketch comedy show that
airs live, Saturday nights, on NBC.

It’s known for having a celebrity guest
host and musical guest for every episode.

Or at least it was until we apparently did
something to anger the gods, and now no one
is allowed to leave their homes anymore.

Eventually they released 3 remotely produced
“quarantine” episodes, and ended the season
early.

But the new seasons of SNL usually begin in
the fall.
And…it’s already August…really?
It’s never gonna end!
Hi, I’m T1J.
(Follow me!)

This video, like most of my videos would not
be possible without my Members and Patrons,

including homies like Otieno Ododa, Tracy
and Sandy, Victor Stone, and Morgan King.

If you’d like to support the channel, you
can become a homie yourself by clicking the

Join button below the video, or by checking
out my page on Patreon.
So, SNL is…fine.

Most of the people who are hired for the show
are already experienced performers and comedians,

so historically the comedy has been solid,
if not mostly safe.

The show has some leeway for off color or
dark humor, due to its late night timeslot,

but generally avoids major controversy with
regard to the content of the sketches.

Which is interesting, given how frequently
SNL makes jokes about politicians or the political
climate of the day.

It’s a long standing tradition for the SNL
cast to lampoon and parody the neverending
lunacy provided to us by American politicians.

In fact, I wouldn’t be surprised if the
resemblance to or ability to impersonate a

relevant politician was major factor in whether
or not you get brought on the show in the
first place.

Early seasons of SNL often commented on social
issues such as poverty,

but in the 80s and 90s the show settled more
into developing its zany recurring characters,
most of whom were decidedly non-political.

But I think most people would agree that the
more recent seasons of SNL have been more
overtly political than ever.

Not only do they impersonate senators and
satirize recent political events as always,

but many of their more recent sketches directly
comment on pressing issues like poverty and
mental health.
And like I said, the show’s fine.

It’s rare that I find myself falling over
laughing or feeling like I’ve just watched
something mind-blowing.

But it happens sometimes, like with the Black
Jeopardy sketches.
Which I think are amazing.

SNL had previously aired a series of popular
Celebrity Jeopardy episodes starring Will

Ferrell as a jaded Alex Trebek reading embarrassingly
easy clues to exaggerated celebrity parodies

who were nevertheless too brainless to ever
get any correct answers.
Black Jeopardy is a little bit different.

The contestants are exaggerations, sure, but
they’re not celebrities, they’re regular
people.

I remember when the first Black Jeopardy sketch
aired.

My first instinct was, “Oh, this is a ripoff
of Dave Chappelle’s ‘I Know Black People’
sketch.”

The fictional game show where contestants
were asked questions about black slang and
black culture.
And maybe there’s some inspiration there.

Part of the humor of the Chappelle sketch
was the inclusion of non-black people, who

were often clueless about black culture, despite
their, often flimsy, connection to the black
community.

Characters such as the Asian convenience store
owner, or the white professor of African American
studies.

The latter actually being an idea that was
rehashed for Black Jeopardy.

But while Chappelle’s game show asked contestants
to recall lyrics from the show Good Times

or identify early 2000’s slang, like “badunkadunk,”
Black Jeopardy zeroed in on a specific, unspoken

understanding that Black Americans often have
with one another, no matter what part of the
country they’re in.

The first Jeopardy clue of the sketch was
the simple phrase, “She think she cute”

The correct reply was of course, “Who is
Monique?”

And I remember laughing unexpectedly at this
the first time I heard it.

It fits in with the absurdist, almost non
sequitur style of comedy that SNL often embraces,

but it also at the same time reflects that
aforementioned mutual understanding.

I knew I was in for a treat at the very beginning
of the sketch, when Kenan Thompson jokingly
introduces himself.

That seems like a joke that some white writer
would have come up with in the 90s or something,

so it kind of set up the fact that this wasn’t
going to be your typical lazy racial humor.

So the set-up of black Jeopardy is that there’s
a black host, Darnell Hayes, played by Kenan

Thompson, who says about the show, “We finally
got our own.”

There are also two black contestants, who
seemed to be completely tuned in to the conceit

of the show, they get every answer correct,
no matter how ambiguous or vague the clues
are.

As I said, this references the shared experiences
and language of black communities.

One thing I like about this sketch, is how
it manages to capture ideas that haven’t
become cliché through overuse in pop culture.

A lot of the language and culture of black
people has been adopted by white people, so

it would be hard to do a real “Black Jeopardy,”
because most everything associated with the

black community is eventually stolen by white
society anyway.

But with clues like, “She do Hair”, and
“Had that been me, I woulda whooped his

ass when he was young;” it really feels
like this stuff was written by actual black

people, and not some white writers overusing
terms like “Flex” and “Lit.”

Ironically though, the Black Jeopardy idea
was conceived by Bryan Tucker, a white guy

who is a co-head writer of SNL, and who incidentally
used to be a writer for Chappelle’s Show,

and actually appeared in the Chappelle Show
sketch I mentioned earlier.

So again, there’s probably some inspiration
there.

Tucker said in an interview with Vulture that
he grew up idolizing black comedians, and

he’s worked on several black shows, like
Chappelle’s Show and The Chris Rock Show,

and he got the idea of Black Jeopardy from
interacting with black people and witnessing

the familiarity they had with one another,
that he lacked.

So he co-wrote the Black Jeopardy sketch with
co-head writer Michael Che, with input from

Kenan Thompson, to try and capture this interesting
dynamic in a funny way.

In the Vulture interview, he said, “If you’re
white, and you’re in these worlds, like

I am a little bit, you’re still not totally
a part of things.

There’s a shared culture if you’re black
that you just have and if you’re white,
you just don’t have.”

And therein lies the main source of humor
of the Black Jeopardy sketches.

In each sketch there is also a third contestant,
who’s a little bit out of sync with the
other contestants.

In the first iteration, this seat is filled
by Mark, a white professor of African American
studies played by Louis C.K.
And yeah I know, it came out in 2014.

The choice of making Mark a professor of African
American studies reflects Tucker’s point

about how even if you feel like you belong
in the conversation, you’ll never be truly

in touch with the shared culture of black
people.

Mark tries to keep up with the black contestants,
Amir, and Keeley, played by Jay Pharoah and

Sasheer Zamata respectively, but can never
quite the get the picture.

Indeed his failure is inevitable, especially
as the Final Jeopardy category is revealed,

“Rap Songs that Begin with the letter N.”
The popularity of Black Jeopardy led to several

sequel sketches, all of which focus on the
shared experience of Black Americans, but

each sketch looks at this idea through a different
lens.

The 2nd sketch features Elizabeth Banks as
Allison, a clearly well-meaning white woman

who claims she “dated a black guy once”
and “doesn’t see color.”

As you might imagine, the game doesn’t go
well for her, until she suddenly trips her
way into a correct answer.

Even though this white lady is clueless about
the black experience, she still gets to win
even though she didn’t learn anything.
I see you SNL.

The third Black Jeopardy sketch was the first
to feature another black person as the third
contestant.
Drake appears as Jared, a Black Canadian.

According to Drake, he was doing an impression
of the guys he grew up with in Toronto with
Afro-Carribean heritage.

Throughout this video, I’ve been talking
about the shared experience of black people,

but really, I’m specifically speaking about
the shared experience of lower middle class

black people in America, which clearly doesn’t
represent the experience of all black people
throughout the world.

This sketch highlights this by having Jared,
a Canadian, be completely on a different page

than Amir and Keeley, even though he’s also
black.

Ultimately they all find common ground when
they realize they share at least one experience,
dealing with prejudice.

Now, the best Black Jeopardy sketch is not
surprisingly the most popular, at least according
to its YouTube viewcount of over 54 million.

The third contestant this time is a MAGA hat
wearing fellow named Doug, played by Tom Hanks,

and at first sight, we’re already cringing
at what this guy might say.

But then delightfully, SNL throws us a curveball,
Doug seems to know all the right answers.

Just like the black contestants, Doug loves
Tyler Perry movies, he doesn’t trust the

fingerprint scanner on the iPhone, and he
thinks the elections are rigged by the illuminati.

As I mentioned before, the idea of Black Jeopardy
is the familiarity that poor and working class

Black Americans have through their shared
experience.

This is evidenced by the “Prizes” that
are mentioned during the sketches, including

“Car Tape” used to patch up a broken car
window or “top foil” used cover kitchen
containers with no lids.

They all invoke images of poverty, which is
something most Black people in America can
relate to either directly or indirectly.

But poverty obviously also affects white people,
and some of those experiences we associate

with black folks are just as much, if not
more, a consequence of class than race.

Having Doug see eye to eye with the black
contestants is an excellent commentary on

how easily people could come together if we
opened ourselves to the idea of listening

to other perspectives and noticed how we all
pretty much want the same things.

We want ourselves and our communities to be
happy and safe and healthy.

There are disagreements on how to do that,
but if we treated each other like complex

human beings, rather than one-dimensional
heroes or villains, it’d be a lot easier
to figure it all out together.

But the sketch ends soberly with the Final
Jeopardy category, “Lives That Matter.”

Despite all of the common ground, Doug and
Darnell will go their separate ways still
thinking of each other as political opponents.

There are two other black Jeopardy sketches
as of today.

The first features Chadwick Boseman playing
his character T’Challa from Black Panther,

and it features a juxtaposition of the working
class Black American to the sensibilities
of a King from a Black Utopia.

It also features, maybe my favorite individual
joke from the whole series.
Raisins.

The most recent Black Jeopardy featured SNL
alum Eddie Murphy who had returned to host

SNL after not appearing on the show for 3
decades.

Murphy was playing Velvet Jones, a character
from 80s era SNL, and really the episode was

mostly fan-service for people who like that
old character.

There is a bit of commentary about how SNL’s
comedy has evolved.

Whereas in the 80s, a pimp making infomercials
selling books teaching women to be prostitutes,
was an edgy and hilarious idea.
Today it seems cringey and out of touch.

Both of these sketches are okay, but I’d
say Black Jeopardy jumped the shark after
the Tom Hanks episode.

While SNL’s sketches are mostly tame, they
have received a lot of criticism for their

frankly inexcusable lack of diversity and
minority representation.

I obviously love a lot of TV shows and movies,
but as a black person it rarely feels like

that media is made with people like me in
mind.

So it was a nice breath of fresh air to see
something on network TV that I actually related
to.

And Black Jeopardy managed to find this interesting
balance between talking about race and class

through a realistic, not stereotypical lens,
while also providing commentary that everyone
can understand.
DAS JUS ME DOE.
What do you think?

I hope you liked my video, but I also wanted
to let you know that in addition to these

videos, I also do a live stream right here
on this channels on Mondays and Fridays, where

I do a variety of things, from games to just
chatting.

I also will be doing a cooking stream soon,
as a celebration of reaching 50 YouTube Members.

I’ve been doing a lot of work trying to
make the stream entertaining and visually

appealing, and one of the tools I’ve been
using is the service provided by this video’s
sponsor Skillshare.

Skillshare is an online learning community
for the curious and the creative, that offers

thousands of classes on various topics including
illustration, music, business, productivity,
and much more.

Whether you’re an excited beginner, a casual
dabbler, or an expert looking to hone their
skills, there’s something there for you.

Now I’ve always considered myself an ideas
guy.

All the video production stuff, I’m king
of just winging it.
You can probably tell.

So I’ve been checking out this great class
on Skillshare from Filmmaker Dandan Liu.

It’s really simple, well organized and straight
to the point, which I think makes it stand
out from other tutorials on the internet.
And that’s kind of how Skillshare is.

Classes are organized into short lessons and
most classes are under 60 minutes total.

So the lessons are kind of designed to fit
any schedule.

And you can get an annual premium membership,
which gives you unlimited access to thousands

of classes like this, as well as a community
of other learners, for less than 10 dollars
a month.

But if you’d like to try it out, the first
1000 of you that click the link in the description

below will get a 2 month free trial of a Skillshare
Premium Membership.

So click the link the description below and
go start learning.

And remember, by supporting sponsors like
Skillshare, you not only get access to a great

service, but you also support me and help
